cards.text: What is the difference between UMN and LMN lesions?
cards.answer: UMN: spastic paralysis, hyperreflexia, positive Babinski, no muscle wasting. LMN: flaccid paralysis, hyporeflexia/areflexia, negative Babinski, muscle wasting and fasciculations

cards.text: What is the Gate Control Theory of pain?
cards.answer: Large-diameter A-beta fibres (touch, pressure) activate inhibitory interneurons in the substantia gelatinosa of the spinal cord dorsal horn, closing the gate to pain signals carried by small C and A-delta fibres

tip.tip: Rubbing a painful area activates the gate

cards.text: What are the functions of the cerebellum?
cards.answer: Coordination of voluntary movements, maintenance of balance and posture, motor learning, and fine-tuning of motor output. Lesions cause ataxia (not paralysis), intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, and nystagmus

tip.tip: Cerebellar lesions affect coordination, not strength

cards.text: Name the main neurotransmitters and their key functions
cards.answer: Acetylcholine (NMJ, parasympathetic), Noradrenaline (sympathetic, alertness), Dopamine (basal ganglia, reward), Serotonin (mood, sleep), GABA (main inhibitory), Glutamate (main excitatory)

Hemisection of the spinal cord causing: ipsilateral UMN paralysis (corticospinal), ipsilateral loss of vibration/proprioception (dorsal column), and contralateral loss of pain/temperature (spinothalamic) below the lesion

tip.tip: Ipsilateral motor + dorsal column, contralateral spinothalamic

cards.text: What is the reticular activating system (RAS)?
cards.answer: A network of neurons in the brainstem reticular formation that maintains consciousness and wakefulness. Damage to the RAS causes coma. It filters sensory input and determines arousal level

cards.text: What are the components of a spinal reflex arc?
cards.answer: Five components: receptor, afferent (sensory) neuron, integration centre (spinal cord), efferent (motor) neuron, and effector (muscle/gland)

cards.text: What is the role of the basal ganglia in motor control?
cards.answer: The basal ganglia initiate and suppress voluntary movements via two pathways: direct pathway (facilitates movement) and indirect pathway (inhibits unwanted movement). Dopamine from substantia nigra modulates both pathways

cards.text: What happens in Wernicke vs Broca aphasia?
cards.answer: Wernicke (posterior, temporal): fluent speech but meaningless (word salad), impaired comprehension. Broca (anterior, frontal): non-fluent speech, effortful, telegraphic, but comprehension preserved

tip.tip: Wernicke = words but no sense, Broca = sense but no words

cards.text: What are the ascending sensory tracts of the spinal cord?
cards.answer: Dorsal column-medial lemniscus (fine touch, vibration, proprioception — ipsilateral ascent, crosses in medulla), Spinothalamic (pain, temperature, crude touch — crosses at spinal level, contralateral ascent), Spinocerebellar (proprioception to cerebellum)

tip.tip: Dorsal column crosses in medulla, spinothalamic crosses in cord
